title: HeartSine Samaritan PAD Series Defibrillator - PAD-Pak Not Recognized or Expired Alert
description: Addresses PAD-Pak not detected or expired alerts caused by installation issues, expired cartridges, or contact/connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the device is needed for patient care, immediately obtain another AED/defibrillator. Do not rely on a unit with a PAD-Pak error.

steps.title: Verify the Alert Condition
steps.instructions: Power on the AED and confirm the exact alert (not recognized vs expired). Observe indicator lights and audible prompts.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Expiration Date (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Remove the PAD-Pak and inspect the expiration date printed on the label. If expired: Replace with a new PAD-Pak → issue resolved.

steps.title: Reseat the PAD-Pak Properly
steps.instructions: Reinsert the PAD-Pak firmly until it is fully seated. Ensure it clicks into place and is not loose.

steps.title: Inspect PAD-Pak and Connector Area
steps.instructions: Check for bent or damaged contacts, debris or contamination, and cracks or damage to the cartridge.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good PAD-Pak
steps.instructions: Install a new or verified working PAD-Pak to determine if the issue is with the cartridge or device.

steps.title: Perform Device Self-Check
steps.instructions: Allow the AED to complete its automatic self-test or initiate one if applicable and observe readiness indicator.

CCR.Complaint: AED displaying PAD-Pak not recognized alert during routine check.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced PAD-Pak with in-date cartridge and verified ready indicator.

helpfulDetails: Indicator light status (green flashing vs fault)
Final device status (in service vs out of service)

title: HeartSine Samaritan PAD Series Defibrillator - Self-Test Failure on Power-On
description: Troubleshooting self-test failure during power-on, typically caused by battery, PAD-Pak, electrode, or environmental issues preventing the AED from entering ready status.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the AED was expected to be used clinically, immediately obtain another working AED. Do not rely on a device that has failed self-test.

steps.title: Verify the Self-Test Failure Indication
steps.instructions: Power on the AED and observe audible tones, voice prompts, status indicator, and any visible error indicators.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Installation (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the PAD-Pak cartridge. Ensure it clicks fully into place and inspect for damage or improper seating.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Expiration and Type
steps.instructions: Verify PAD-Pak is not expired and is the correct type. Replace with a known-good, in-date PAD-Pak if questionable.

steps.title: Inspect Electrode Connection (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure electrode leads are intact with no visible damage and are properly connected internally.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Assess for excessive heat, cold, moisture, or condensation and allow device to stabilize at room temperature if needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle the AED
steps.instructions: Turn the device off and back on, observing if the self-test re-runs and passes.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good PAD-Pak (Definitive External Test)
steps.instructions: Install a confirmed working PAD-Pak to determine if the issue resolves or persists.

CCR.Complaint: AED failed self-test on power-on and did not show ready status.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced PAD-Pak with in-date unit; AED passed self-test and returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Status indicator behavior (green flashing, red, or none)
Audible tones or voice prompts noted
Final device status (in service or removed)

title: HeartSine Samaritan PAD Series Defibrillator - Shock Delivery or Charging Failure
description: Failure to charge or deliver shock due to PAD-Pak, pads, or internal system faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the device fails during an emergency, switch to a backup defibrillator and continue CPR per protocol.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the AED, observe charge tones, prompts, and whether the shock button activates.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Installation (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Reseat PAD-Pak, ensure proper latch, and verify expiration date.

steps.title: Inspect Electrode Pad Condition and Connection
steps.instructions: Check for proper connection, damage, expiration, or prior use.

steps.title: Check for Audible and Visual Prompts
steps.instructions: Listen for analysis vs charging prompts and observe LED/status indicators.

steps.title: Verify Battery Capability (PAD-Pak Condition)
steps.instructions: Check expiration and replace with known-good PAD-Pak if needed.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Storage Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure proper temperature and no moisture or contamination.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Reattempt Operation
steps.instructions: Restart device and retest operation.

CCR.Complaint: AED failed to charge and did not deliver shock during readiness check.
CCR.Cause: Expired PAD-Pak with insufficient battery capacity prevented charging.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced PAD-Pak with new unit and verified successful charge and shock prompt during test.

title: HeartSine Samaritan PAD Series Defibrillator - Audio Prompt Failure
description: No voice prompts, low volume, or unclear audio during operation, which may prevent proper user guidance during emergency response.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the device is needed for a patient, immediately replace it with another AED. Do not rely on a unit with failed audio prompts. Expected: Patient care continues with a functioning device Why it matters: Audio prompts are critical for safe AED operation, especially for untrained users

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the AED and listen for voice prompts. Observe: No sound at all Distorted or faint audio Intermittent audio output Expected: Clear, audible voice prompts during startup Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is consistent or intermittent

steps.title: Check Environment for Noise Interference
steps.instructions: Move the device to a quiet area and repeat the test. Expected: Audio should be clearly audible in a normal environment Why it matters: Loud environments can be mistaken for audio failure

steps.title: Inspect Speaker Area
steps.instructions: Check the speaker opening for: Dirt or debris Tape, labels, or obstruction Physical damage Clean gently if needed. Expected: Speaker opening is clear and unobstructed Why it matters: Blocked speakers can significantly reduce or eliminate sound output

steps.title: Check Battery / PAD-Pak Status
steps.instructions: Verify the installed PAD-Pak is: Properly seated Not expired Not showing a replace indicator Replace with a known good PAD-Pak if available. Expected: Device powers on normally with full audio prompts Why it matters: Low or failing battery can affect speaker performance

steps.title: Observe Status Indicator
steps.instructions: Check the device status LED (ready indicator). Green = Ready Red or flashing = fault condition Expected: Green ready indicator Why it matters: Internal faults may include audio subsystem issues

steps.title: Perform a Functional Self-Test (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Allow the unit to complete its self-test cycle or initiate a manual test if available. Expected: No errors and normal audio prompts Why it matters: Confirms whether the device detects internal faults

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Unit (If Available)
steps.instructions: Compare behavior with another Samaritan PAD unit. Expected: Known good unit produces normal audio Why it matters: Helps isolate device-specific failure vs user perception

CCR.Complaint: AED reported with no audible voice prompts during routine check.
CCR.Cause: Internal speaker failure confirmed after verifying unobstructed speaker, good PAD-Pak, and failed audio during self-test.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service, labeled out of service, and sent to vendor for repair evaluation.

title: HeartSine Samaritan PAD Series Defibrillator - Ready Indicator or Status LED Errors
description: Troubleshooting when the status LED is not flashing green, shows red, or indicates the AED is not ready for use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the AED is needed for an active emergency and is not showing ready: Immediately retrieve a backup AED. Do not delay patient care while troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify LED Status Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe the status indicator: Flashing green = ready. Flashing red or no light = fault condition.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Installation (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Open the device and ensure the PAD-Pak is fully seated. Remove and reinstall firmly until it clicks into place.

steps.title: Check PAD-Pak Expiration Date
steps.instructions: Inspect expiration date on PAD-Pak. Replace if expired or near expiration.

steps.title: Inspect for Shipping Tab or Activation Issue
steps.instructions: Ensure any battery isolation tab has been removed. Confirm device has been activated per initial setup.

steps.title: Perform a Manual Power-On Self-Test
steps.instructions: Turn the AED ON and observe prompts. Listen for voice prompts and watch LED behavior.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure device is within proper temperature range. Inspect for moisture, dust, or contamination.

steps.title: Inspect Device for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, water ingress, or impact damage. Check speaker grille and casing integrity.

steps.title: Replace PAD-Pak if Issue Persists
steps.instructions: Install a new, known-good PAD-Pak.

CCR.Complaint: AED status indicator not flashing green and showing fault condition.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced PAD-Pak; device passed self-test and returned to ready status.

helpfulDetails: Whether PAD-Pak was reseated or replaced
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)
